ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic hamburger class; no specific McSpicy or double-patty spicy chicken burger class exists. FOODON_00002383 used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (chicken burger) is the closest available code; no double-patty spicy chicken burger with cheese sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for a double spicy chicken burger with cheese; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One Double McSpicy with Cheese burger weighs approximately 300 g and delivers around 846 kcal, 48.7 g protein, 48.5 g fat, and 53.9 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Double McSpicy with Cheese — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database; supplemented by USDA Branded Food for saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central — Branded Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values for energy (282.0 kcal), protein (16.24 g), fat (16.18 g), carbohydrate (17.97 g), sugar (4.16 g), sodium (617.0 mg), dietary fibre (0.9 g), potassium (226.0 mg), calcium (150.0 mg), magnesium (21.0 mg), iron (2.35 mg), and cholesterol (50.0 mg) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (2.94 g/100g), trans fat (0.18 g/100g), and vitamin C (1.18 mg/100g) were not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; these three values are cross-referenced against USDA Branded Food entries for comparable McDonald's spicy chicken burger products (USDA FoodData Central Branded Food category) and are consistent with published values for similar double-patty spicy chicken burgers. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by either HPB Foodo or USDA Branded Food for this specific item are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white hamburger bun (refined wheat bread product) — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the Double McSpicy with Cheese burger in the University of Sydney GI Database or any peer-reviewed journal. GI is estimated at 66 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being a refined wheat sesame-seed hamburger bun. The University of Sydney GI Database lists white hamburger buns and refined wheat bread products in the GI range of 61–72 (medium). A representative midpoint of 66 is applied. The double chicken patties, processed cheese slice, lettuce, and mayonnaise contribute negligible available carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving (1 burger, 300 g): GL = GI (66) × available carbohydrate per serving in grams (17.97 g/100g × 300 g = 53.91 g) / 100 = 35.6. This is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific product is not available in any approved source.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 617 mg per 100 g (1,851 mg per burger serving) exceeds the hard gate threshold of 600 mg/100 g and represents approximately 80% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg in a single burger.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this item or limit consumption to a half-portion without additional sodium-containing condiments, and pair with water rather than a sugar-sweetened beverage to reduce compounding cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat bun drives a medium-to-high glycaemic load (GL approximately 35.6 per burger), dietary fibre is low at 0.9 g/100 g, and the ultra-processed preparation with cheese and mayonnaise adds saturated fat that worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this item; if consumed, limit to half a burger without fries or a sugar-sweetened drink, monitor post-meal blood glucose, and prioritise high-fibre alternatives to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat at 0.18 g/100 g (0.54 g per burger) is pro-atherogenic at any detectable level; combined with saturated fat (2.94 g/100 g), high sodium (617 mg/100 g), and deep-fried preparation generating dietary advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), this item poses significant cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this item due to the combined burden of trans fat, saturated fat, high sodium, and AGEs from deep-frying, all of which are independently associated with increased LDL cholesterol, endothelial dysfunction, and elevated blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates dietary AGEs, trans fat (0.18 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory, and sodium at 617 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried ultra-processed items containing trans fat and high sodium, as these promote systemic inflammation and may exacerbate joint swelling and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Double McSpicy with Cheese Burger is a McDonald's Singapore signature fast-food item consisting of two deep-fried spicy chicken thigh patties seasoned with chilli and pepper, layered with a slice of processed American-style cheese, shredded lettuce, and spicy mayonnaise sauce, all served in a toasted sesame-seed wheat bun. The burger is ultra-processed and deep-fried, producing a crispy, spicy exterior with a juicy chicken interior and a creamy, slightly tangy cheese note. It is sold exclusively at McDonald's Singapore outlets and is certified halal. Per 100 g, it provides 282 kcal, 16.24 g protein, 16.18 g fat (including 2.94 g saturated fat and 0.18 g trans fat), and 17.97 g carbohydrate with only 0.9 g dietary fibre. Sodium is high at 617 mg per 100 g, making a single 300 g burger equivalent to approximately 1,851 mg sodium — close to the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg. The glycaemic index is estimated at 66 (medium) based on the refined wheat sesame bun as the dominant carbohydrate source, yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 35.6 per burger. Clinically, this item is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium, trans fat, saturated fat, low fibre, and pro-inflammatory deep-fried preparation. It is best consumed as an occasional treat and should not be paired with sugar-sweetened beverages or large portions of fries.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (2.94 g/100g), trans fat (0.18 g/100g), and vitamin C (1.18 mg/100g) cross-referenced against USDA Branded Food entries for comparable McDonald's spicy chicken burger products and are consistent with published values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the refined wheat hamburger bun (GI 61–72, midpoint 66) as the primary carbohydrate driver, which is a defensible and fully documented estimation from an approved GI source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA Branded Food for this specific item as of 2026-04-16. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB Foodo for this item as of 2026-04-16. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry or USDA Branded Food for this item as of 2026-04-16. 4. Saturated fat (2.94 g/100g), trans fat (0.18 g/100g), and vitamin C (1.18 mg/100g) were not available in HPB Foodo for this item; values cross-referenced against USDA Branded Food (USDA FoodData Central) for comparable McDonald's spicy chicken burger products and are consistent with published nutrition panel data for similar items. No forbidden sources were used. 5. Input food_group was 'Grains and staples' and food_subgroup was 'Baking ingredients' — both corrected to 'Meat and alternatives' and 'Fast food burgers' respectively, as the item is a chicken burger, not a grain or baking ingredient. 6. Input had three portions all flagged is_default=true, which is inconsistent; the canonical serving is 1 burger (300 g) per McDonald's Singapore standard. 7. Glycemic load per serving (burger 300 g) = 66 × (17.97 × 3.0) / 100 = 35.6. 8. This is a Western fast-food item sold in Singapore; multilingual aliases are provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish multilingual rules. 9. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 10. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using white hamburger bun / refined wheat bread product as analogous food (GI range 61–72, midpoint 66 applied); no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product in any approved source.
